data.title: How The U.S. Treasury Can Cash In Big Using Its Gold Revaluation Account
data.description:
The U.S. Treasury can draw up to $700 billion in new funding from its gold revaluation account at the Federal Reserve. And the Treasury could invest this "new money" in a sovereign wealth fund (increasing the money supply by an equal amount).

Using its gold revaluation account would emphasize gold's strength versus the dollar, something the United States government has been trying to downplay for decades. This turn of events would be bullish for gold and weaken the dollar.

Introduction

I started writing about central bank gold revaluation accounts (GRAs) to show how they can use a trick to write off assets, such as government bonds, from their balance sheet with new money from their GRAs. The German central bank even wrote to me in an email that it doesn't rule out this possibility for the future.

Then I discovered how central banks have been using their GRAs to absorb losses, like the Central Bank of Curaçao and Saint Martin (CBCS) did in 2021. Coincidentally, as interest rates went up in 2022 and central banks globally experienced shortfalls, officials from the Dutch and German central banks commented that their massive GRAs underline the soundness of their balance sheets. Providing a solvency backstop, if you will.

Because of how modern central bank balance sheets are structured, GRAs can also be used to fund their respective Treasuries. Even the World Bank noted my writings, as it referenced my research on GRAs in a handbook for asset managers on why to invest in gold.

What Is a Gold Revaluation Account, and How Can It Be Used?

A gold revaluation account (GRA) is an accounting item on the liability side of a central bank balance sheet that records unrealized gains of gold assets.

When the price of gold denominated in fiat currency rises, as it inevitably does in the long run, gold assets increase in value, and concurrently, the GRA swells.

As a formula:

GRA = present gold market value - gold purchasing cost

In this example, central bank X once bought gold for $300, and the present value of that gold has gone up to $1,500, creating a GRA of $1,200. Capital and the Gold Revaluation Account is the central bank's equity.

In many textbooks, you will read GRAs are meant to cushion a decline in the price of gold, but because gold is scarce and the denominator (fiat) on central banks' balance sheets is not-visible in large GRAs of banks that own gold for a long time-there are far more creative possibilities for it.

In the example below, I have illustrated that a central bank can transfer entries from its GRA to its own capital position and absorb losses (+200); to the government's checking account held at the central bank (+100); or to write off assets (-500).

It's just numbers.

If central bank X draws $800 from its GRA, there is still (1200 - 800) $400 left to cushion a potential decline in the price of gold.

The American Setup

Take notice that the central bank of the United States owns not one ounce of gold. With the Gold Reserve Act of 1934, the Federal Reserve transferred all its monetary gold to the Treasury in return for a special series of gold certificates valued at $35 an ounce. These gold certificates are purely an accounting item and can't be redeemed for gold.

Because the U.S. has always been reluctant to raise the statutory price of the gold in its books to downplay the strength of gold versus the dollar, the Fed's gold certificates today are still valued at 42.22 dollars an ounce, a price approved by Congress during the demise of Bretton Woods in the early 1970s.

On February 3, 2025, President Trump signed an executive order for the creation of a sovereign wealth fund within one year. Standing next to him when he signed the order was Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, who told reporters, "We're going to monetize the asset side of the U.S. balance sheet for the American people."

What asset can that be? He could be referring to selling off federal lands or vacant government office buildings, but the Financial Times speculated on February 7 that it's a reference to gold:

Some hedge fund contemporaries of Scott Bessent, the hedgie-turned-US Treasury secretary, are speculating about a revaluation of America's gold stocks.

This week, such chatter intensified after Bessent both pledged to "monetise the asset side of the US balance sheet" - in other words, to focus on assets as much as liabilities - while also promising to lower 10-year Treasury yields.

Perhaps the Trump administration does want to draw on the Treasury's gold revaluation account at the Fed, as happened in 1972 when the official gold price was raised from $35 to $38, and in 1973 from $38 to $42.22 an ounce.

Doing so would allow the Treasury to spend hundreds of billions of dollars without increasing the national debt.

Step one would be for Congress to approve an increase in the official price of gold. Step two is for the Treasury to issue new gold certificates for the Federal Reserve.

Once the value of the Fed's gold certificates on its balance sheet goes up, the Treasury's checking account at the Fed, commonly referred to as the Treasury General Account (TGA), increases by the same amount.

As the Fed doesn't own any gold, a windfall from the revaluation of its gold certificates is for the owner of the physical gold, which is the Treasury. Note that when GRAs are used for spending, it creates new money and thus has to be done through central banks. Welcome to the wonderful world of accounting!

The below illustration is from a paper by Albert E. Burger, published by the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis in 1974: "The Monetary Economics of Gold." When, in 1972, the statutory price of gold was revalued to $38 from $35 an ounce, the TGA increased by roughly $800 million.

H/t John Paul Koning. The same procedure is described in the Financial Accounting Manual for Federal Reserve Banks, published in April 2024 (page 12).

From A. E. Burger:

On May 15, 1972, the Treasury took steps to monetize the increased value of the gold stock. This occurred as follows: the Treasury issued to the Federal Reserve Banks gold certificates equal to the increased official dollar value of the gold stock and, in return, the Treasury received from the Federal Reserve an increase of an equal amount in its deposits at the Federal Reserve Banks [TGA].

Following the revaluation of the U.S. monetary gold in 1972, the Treasury spent $800 million on the private sector-without selling an ounce of gold-increasing the monetary base (reserves) and broad money supply (deposits) by an equal amount.

How Much Can Trump Spend?

Currently, the Treasury owns 8,133 tonnes of gold valued at $42.22 an ounce, which equals $11 billion, while the prevailing market value of the gold is roughly $750 billion.

The Treasury can deplete its gold revaluation account via the Fed in full but then runs the risk that the free market price of gold falls below the new U.S. statutory price, which would undercollateralize America's balance sheet.

So, either Trump draws less than $750 billion from this trick, or he takes it all and puts a floor under the free market price of gold, which brings us one step closer to a gold standard. This, of course, would be bullish for gold as its role in the system would be promoted to the highest tier once again.

The Federal Reserve could counter the Treasury's expansion of the monetary base by selling bonds, but this would drive up interest rates, which is not what Trump wants. Trump, firmly in charge since in office, is thought to want a weaker dollar to boost exports.

Expanding the monetary base by drawing on the United States' GRA would do just that and allow him to invest in a sovereign fund. Two birds with one stone.

Sources:

Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System (2024): Financial Accounting Manual for Federal Reserve Banks

Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis (1974): The Monetary Economics of Gold

Financial Times (2025): Gold glitters as the unimaginable becomes imaginable

Myrmikan Capital, LLC (2025): Gold Revaluation

Nieuwenhuijs, Jan (2022-2024): German Central Bank Doesn't Rule Out Gold Revaluation, Governor Dutch Central Bank States Gold Revaluation Account Is Solvency Backstop, How a Central Bank in the Caribbean Recently Used Its Gold Revaluation Account to Cover Losses, German Central Bank: Gold Revaluation Account Underlines Soundness of Balance Sheet, How Central Banks Can Use Gold Revaluation Accounts in Times of Financial Stress, World Bank Report Highlights Advantage of Central Bank Gold Revaluation Accounts

Reuters (2025): Trump orders creation of US sovereign wealth fund, says it could buy TikTok

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Upwork: Impressive Profit Growth, But The Longer Term Is Shaky
data.description:
Though we've likely tired of hearing AI-related headlines by now, the harsh reality is that the rapid advance of AI technology is going to impact a sea change in our modern workforces within the next few years. And for many companies, that represents a tremendous opportunity to right-size corporate headcount and manage costs: but for others, AI is a very disruptive force.

To me, Upwork (NASDAQ:UPWK) is one of the companies that stands to be disrupted. The freelance work marketplace has executed tremendously well so far, and its recent Q4 earnings print helped to extend a ~50% rally in the stock that has taken place since October. But the question for investors now is: can the good times last?

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Upwork in November, when the stock was trading at similar ~$16 levels. Since then, Upwork stock is attempting a new breakout rally on the back of very strong Q4 results, which showcased the company's profitability chops following its decision to lay off a good chunk of its employees last October. And yet, given Upwork's fair valuation and the longer-term risks that AI poses, I remain neutral on Upwork's prospects.

Understandably, investors are excited about the potential for AI growth here, and Upwork has gone to great lengths to demonstrate that it's a beneficiary of the trend. In Q4, the company reported that total GSV (gross services volume, or the total dollar amount of jobs booked on the Upwork platform) from AI-related projects rose 33% y/y.

The company has also released its own proprietary AI tool, branded "Uma," which it describes as a "work companion" that helps make smart search and recommendations (it seems that almost every software company has released some version of an AI tool like this, often billed as either a companion or a copilot). The company also made a small tuck-in acquisition of a company called Objective that specializes in AI native search.

And yet to me, long-term risks abound. Upwork may be touting huge 30%+ growth rates in AI GSV - but at the same time, its revenue growth has decelerated to the single digits, well below the ~20% growth clip that Upwork had enjoyed at the height of the pandemic, when companies were hybridizing their workforces and relied on freelance or contract labor to fill missing holes.

To me, this suggests that while it's true that Upwork is winning AI-related jobs, it may also be losing a lot of the simpler jobs that are its bread and butter (think of jobs such as editing, web design, translation - many of these functions either can today or eventually will be replaced by AI). Current data suggests that AI is not a net growth driver for Upwork.

With this in mind, I continue to remain on the sidelines for Upwork: while the company's current profit expansion is quite impressive, it's unclear whether this strength can last.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Upwork's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Upwork's total revenue grew only 4% y/y to $191.5 million, though this beat Wall Street's expectations of $180.4 million (-2% y/y) by a huge margin. Still, we note a number of disappointing factors: revenue growth decelerated sharply from 10% y/y growth in Q3. Second, the company's GSV also declined -7% y/y to just $992.8 million, which was worse than a -3% y/y decline in GSV in Q3. Again: how can the company tout AI as a major growth driver, when its growth rates have only slipped since AI began revolutionizing the workforce?

Unsurprisingly, the company blamed its weakness on macro conditions. The company nots that the staffing industry is sitting at multiyear lows. Per CFO Erica Gessert's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

And when macroeconomic conditions improve, these catalysts will be additional growth drivers for us. As we head into 2025, our outlook reflects our caution with regard to the current macroeconomic environment.

While some of the indicators we watch for our business, such as the Jolts report and other macro data showed signs of stabilization in Q4, they remain at multi-year lows. Historically, these indicators tend to have a six to nine-month lag effect on our business. The current macro uncertainty makes us cautious about the outlook for 2025, but despite that Upwork continues to perform well and we are executing across every area of our business."

To me, however, this is hopeful thinking. While it's true that macro conditions have impacted enterprise budgets, some of the staffing reductions that companies are making may prove to be permanent, especially if AI proves to be a reasonable replacement.

We do, however, appreciate Upwork's major strides in profitability amid tougher top-line conditions. The company's adjusted EBITDA soared to $50.2 million in the quarter, representing a 26.2% margin, jumping 960bps versus a 16.6% margin in the year-ago quarter.

The company here benefited from its decision to cut ~20% of its headcount in October, while also updating its pricing and improving its take rates on its GSV. And yet we note that the fiscal discipline that Upwork is showcasing for its own workforce may be exactly the trend that other companies are replicating, which is directly impacting Upwork's ability to advance its GSV.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices near $17, Upwork trades at a market cap of $2.22 billion. After we net off the $622.1 million of cash and $357.9 million of debt on Upwork's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $1.96 billion.

Meanwhile, for the current fiscal year FY25, Upwork has guided to $740-$760 million in revenue, which represents a decline of -1% to -4%, and adjusted EBITDA of $180-$190 million - or a 24.7% margin at the midpoint that represents 10% y/y growth.

As such, Upwork trades at 10.6x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA. The stock is neither cheap nor expensive: but we have to note that this valuation multiple reflects a company that is expecting declines in GSV and revenue, as well as long-run risks stemming from AI that may make GSV growth quite difficult to achieve at all, even when macro conditions subside.

To me, this is not the right stock to bank on: steer clear and invest elsewhere.

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Markets Care More About Income
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company ("BDC") sector from both the bottom-up - highlighting individual news and events - as well as the top-down - providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the first week of February.

Market Action

BDCs underperformed this week with a total return of around -1% even as nearly all other income sectors were up. The sector took its cue from equities which were also down while the rise in Treasuries buoyed the rest of the income space.

The average valuation in our coverage fell back somewhat, though it remains above its longer-term average.

Market Themes

This early earnings season has highlighted one important theme, which is that markets care much more about income than they do about overall performance (i.e. total NAV or book value return).

We saw this with ARCC where Q4 earnings missed the consensus estimate of $0.58 and came in at $0.55. The stock fell by 3% on the news. We also saw it with GBDC, which delivered net investment income of $0.39, missing by $0.04. The stock fell by over 2%.

What's interesting here is that in the case of ARCC, the NAV rose by 12 cents - 4x the amount by which net investment income disappointed. And while the GBDC NAV fell, the total NAV return exceeded that of the previous quarter. In fact, both companies delivered strong absolute total NAV return numbers - 13% and 11.7% annualized figures. Both are well above the longer-term average return in our coverage. In the case of ARCC this was the average of its last 2 quarters and for GBDC it was stronger than its previous quarter.

In our view, the negative market reaction was myopic. As we have discussed a number of times, investors should care much more about the total NAV return rather than the quarterly noise around the income level. Apart from income, the former also captures broader portfolio performance, such as realized and unrealized gains and losses. These non-income performance drivers differ much more widely across the different BDCs, and it is they that ultimately determine the overall wealth that investors will generate on their capital.

Market Commentary

Oaktree Specialty Lending Corp (OCSL) put up another disappointing quarter with a total NAV return of 0.5%. Absent the fee waiver, the result would have been flat. The wheels aren’t quite coming off the company like we saw with TPVG and HRZN as total NAV returns have not dipped into the negative territory. However, it doesn’t look great.

The stock closed the week at a 12% discount (vs. flat for the median BDC in coverage). The company is putting in new shareholder-friendly features which are very good to see however they are not sufficient to turn performance around. At the end of the day, it’s the credit performance of the portfolio (including underwriting quality as well as the management of positions and workouts) that is the key driver of performance, rather than yield or various waivers which can be helpful but are second-order.

The company waived most of the incentive fees this quarter, adding about 0.5% to the quarter’s return. This was a result of putting in a total return hurdle, meaning the incentive fee will consider overall capital gains and losses. About a third of BDCs in coverage have a total return hurdle, so that is a welcome move.

The dividend was cut from $0.55 to $0.47, something we highlighted in our last update was the likely outcome of its recent income trend. The dividend is the sum of a $0.40 base dividend and a $0.07 supplemental. Given net investment income moved below the dividend, the company’s hand was forced. Unlike CEFs, BDC’s don’t like to pay out more in dividends than they earn. In fact, they try to keep dividend coverage above 100% as many view retained income as cheap equity despite the excise tax on the under-distribution.

Capital Southwest (CSWC) reported Q4 results. Total NAV return was a strong 3.9%. Non-accruals fell to 2.7% from a pretty high 3.5% last quarter. The NAV remained unchanged. Net investment income fell about 3% on the quarter, covering the base dividend by 6 cents. Accretion from issuance of new stock drove a $0.27 gain or an additional 1.6% return. Absent this, the total NAV return would have been a subpar 2.3%. The company continues to generate above-average returns only because of the additional equity issuance at the premium valuation. If the company’s premium disappears, it would be a below-average BDC. This makes it a fairly fragile holding.

Golub BDC (GBDC) posted a solid result with a 2.8% total NAV return last quarter (NAV fell by 0.4%). A realized loss as a result of non-accrual investment restructuring was offset by some unrealized appreciation. The company has had an average of 0.7% realized losses per quarter over the past year. That’s worth keeping an eye on, but it’s not a dealbreaker in the context of the overall return.

Non-accruals fell to 0.5% on fair value - well below the average in coverage. Net investment income came in at $0.39 vs. $0.47 in the last quarter. This looks to be due to an increase in incentive fees (a function of the return generated), a jump in interest expense (due to debt extinguishment and a new securitization) and a drop in income. There are likely some timing issues here as well as the debt load grew but was likely not put to work immediately. The valuation is decent for a starter position at a 1% premium - pretty much in line with the median BDC in coverage.

data.title: Carbon Countdown
data.description:
By Maya Beyhan

The energy transition represents a crucial market force with the potential to revolutionize our world. Over the two decades from Jan. 31, 2005, to Jan. 31, 2025, the S&P 500® demonstrated a significant decarbonization, evidenced by a 73.9% decrease in its weighted average carbon intensity, as illustrated in Exhibit 1.1 This material decrease highlights a shift in dynamics among the S&P 500’s constituents.

Central to this decarbonization is the growing adoption of renewable energy sources alongside a reduction in the use of fossil fuels. By utilizing S&P Global Trucost’s Environmental data suite, we analyzed the energy consumption of S&P 500 constituents based on energy source, tracking changes in this energy mix over the past decade.1 The findings of this analysis are summarized in Exhibit 2, which outlines the significant shifts in energy consumption patterns observed in recent years. Over the past decade, the role of renewables — consisting of hydroelectric, biomass and other renewables components — in the S&P 500’s energy mix has more than doubled, jumping from 95.7k GWh to 200.6k GWh.

In contrast to the growth in renewables, the consumption of fossil fuel-based energy — consisting of coal, petroleum and natural gas — has declined by 8.8% within the S&P 500, with petroleum usage nearly reduced to a quarter of its previous level, dropping from 16.5k GWh to 4.4k GWh (see Exhibit 2). This transition toward cleaner energy may be indicative of a market that is adapting to new economic conditions and embracing innovation.

In summary, the energy transition appears to be affecting S&P 500 constituents, and this serves as an indicator of how decarbonization is reshaping market dynamics. The insights derived from observing the S&P 500 during this transformative phase underscore the path the world is on as the energy landscape continues to evolve. For those interested in further examining the carbon metrics and fossil fuel reserves associated with S&P DJI’s indices, additional details can be found in the Sustainability Index Dashboard.

1 Analysis carried out using Portfolio Analytics on S&P Capital IQ Pro.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit S&P Dow Jones Indices. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: 3 Reasons I Am Bullish On Ollie's Bargain Outlet And 1 Reason To Be Hesitant
data.description:
American consumers are taking a more cautious approach to spending in 2025. Recent economic news and data certainly highlights some of the potential root causes, from fears of inflation reigniting to sprawling tariffs, U.S. consumer sentiment recently plunged to a seven-month low in early February 2025. Unfortunately, with new tariff policies going into effect and signs of inflation already rearing its ugly head, consumers could be in for bumpy ride in the year to ahead.

February’s CPI inflation report showed an unexpected surge of 0.5% on a month-over-month basis, compared to economist estimates of only a 0.3% increase. Meanwhile, President Trump says his administration is exploring retaliatory tariff measures that could be implemented to nations around the world for their response to the Administration’s initial tariff implementation.

For consumers, this means potentially higher prices on everything from staples to discretionary purchases. According to a recent survey from Smarty, an online shopping rewards app, nearly half of U.S. respondents say they plan to deal with expected price hikes by simply buying less. Another 40% of surveyors say they will search for bargains and switch to cheaper brands in order to find shelter from pricing increases.

As a result of expected rising prices and shifting consumer habits, retail stocks could be in for a tough road ahead. In fact, the Washington Post recently reported that U.S. retail store closures could exceed 15,000 this year, which would be nearly double from 2024.

However, some retail companies could be net benefactors from the tariff, inflation and consumer spending habit changes that lie ahead. Companies like Ollie’s Bargain Outlet Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:OLLI) have the right business model to survive and even potentially thrive in this environment.

Here are three reasons that I am bullish and one reason I am hesitant on Ollie’s right now:

Bullish #1: Tariff Opportunity to Expand Sales and Store Growth

During the company’s fiscal third quarter earnings call (December 2024), management made some surprising statements that many other retail executives certainly are not sharing right now. Specifically, Ollie’s management says they see tariffs as a potential benefit for the company because it would provide more opportunities to acquire closeout items cheaply from competitor store closures.

However, Ollie’s will not be completely immune from global trade wars. Management disclosed that 15% of its product flow are direct China imports. With China being a key nation within the emerging global trade war, this exposure is certainly very important to keep in mind.

Given Ollie’s business model of acquiring closeouts, liquidations, etc., the perceived rough patch ahead for the retail industry could lead to many opportunities to grow their inventory of items at a fair price, which in turn, allows the company to pass savings onto their loyal customer base.

Remember, experts are already estimating double the store closures for 2025 compared to last year. This provides a robust opportunity for Ollie’s to grow sales and further expand its footprint within the United States.

In the last year, Ollie’s has been very successful with implementing its growth playbook of acquiring closeout items and store properties from declining competitors. Specifically, the company acquired 11 99 Cents Only Stores out of bankruptcy last May and an additional 17 closing Big Lots stores were acquired later in 2024.

Keep in mind though Ollie’s management says they have seen competition increase with these closing stores while they are engaging in store-closing sales and liquidations. During this brief period, bargain-conscious shoppers have been picking over closing stores in the hopes of securing deals. However, once those locations officially close, that is when Ollie’s can go in and acquire any additional leftover inventory and/or store locations.

Bullish #2: Expansion Opportunity

As of its fiscal third quarter 2024 earnings call, Ollie’s had a total of 550 stores across 31 states. Executives issued their guidance for 2025, targeting an additional 56 new stores opening. If this target is met, it would represent a record year for new store openings for the company.

Headquartered in Pennsylvania, Ollie’s has really focused its initial efforts on building a presence in the East Coast. However, management has set its sights on expanding westward, especially into the Midwest and Plains regions. In preparation for their western expansion, the company recently completed construction of a new, state-of-the-art distribution center located in Princeton, Illinois. In total, the Princeton DC facility and other DCs have the capacity to service up to an estimated 750 stores, which provides massive opportunity for the company to support its store growth in the coming years.

What is also exciting about this new Illinois DC is the fact that management says there are still opportunities to expand the facility to accommodate additional stores once they reach that point. As a result, they do not see the need to engage in any additional extensive capex projects, like building additional distribution centers, in the near future.

For investors, this is absolutely huge. First off, the company has a brand-new distribution facility that, in combination with other existing warehouses, can support an additional 200 new stores from current levels. Furthermore, management’s engaging in low-capex expansion opportunities to that facility could support further store expansion opportunities without the need to build additional expensive distribution centers.

Bullish #3: Benefitting From the Cautious Consumer and Loyal Membership Base

As I stated earlier, U.S. consumers are already changing their spending habits in anticipation of tariffs and resurging inflation. With consumers being more selective and looking for bargains, it makes sense that retailers like Ollie’s and TJ Maxx could be major benefactors.

From clothes and kitchen items to pets, sporting goods and beyond, Ollie’s really has an extensive offering that can serve just about any customer. As a result, the company has really built up a loyal customer base. In fact, the company’s membership program, Ollie’s Army, saw an 8% increase during its most recent quarter to new total of 14.8 million shoppers. Ollie’s Army members represent around 80% of the company’s total sales.

Even more interesting is the fact that management’s aggressive digital marketing and outreach to reach Millennials and younger shoppers appears to have really paid off. Management in the recent earnings call noted they are seeing very strong adoption from customers aged 18-45.

As Ollie’s plans its store expansion westward, they have a proven marketing strategy that could help further expand their membership base among younger shoppers. This is important because by establishing these relationships early, Ollie’s could really benefit from life-long customers to continue driving sales into the future.

Ollie’s is also currently experimenting with a credit card, which is currently in a testing phase across 70 store locations in its home state of Pennsylvania. Although the credit card product is still very new and early, management was optimistic to see approvals and spending higher than their initial estimates.

It will take the rest of 2025 to implement a larger rollout of the credit card program, but this could be another interesting development to keep an eye on. Not only does it provide another source for revenue, but it also can be another tool to build customer loyalty and engagement.

Reality Check: Ollie’s Stock is Expensive

Although I have highlighted a lot of reasons to be bullish on Ollie’s, there is still one very big reason that makes me hesitant: the stock is very expensive. As of this writing, Ollie’s trades at a forward P/E of 28.52x and a forward EV/EBITDA of 19.51x. To be fair, TJ Maxx parent company, TJX Companies, has similarly expensive ratios. Forward P/E is listed at nearly 28x and forward EV/EBITDA stands at 19x for the company, as of this publication.

This shows that investors have certainly been bidding up shares of Ollie’s and TJX, likely for a lot of the same reasons that are highlighted in this article. For context, the S&P 500 has a current P/E of just over 25x. This means Ollie’s is more expensive on a price-to-earnings basis than the overall market. Although I like Ollie’s outlook and positioning, I find it very difficult to buy at these levels.

Instead, I would focus on getting into Ollie’s on reasonable pullbacks. If we see the market sell off as a result of inflation worries or tariff developments, shares of Ollie’s could see a pullback as well. However, I do not expect any of these pullbacks to last long for the bargain retailer, as investors will see the opportunity to jump in.

Overall, Ollie’s Bargain Outlet is a compelling retail opportunity that should perform well from a growth standpoint. The company’s business model is set up to benefit from turbulence in the retail industry, as it provides opportunities to buy closeout and liquidation inventory cheaply and then offer its customers those savings on everyday items.

As consumers increase their scrutiny of pricing increases, Ollie’s stands to potentially see meaningful store traffic in the year ahead. Factor in management’s successful outreach and connection to younger shoppers, which certainly can be replicated in other parts of the U.S. as the store expansion continues to rollout.

Although Ollie’s Bargain Outlet is a compelling growth story, investors have sent shares surging higher in the past year. As a result, the stock is simply too expensive for me to jump in at current levels. Instead, I am placing OLLI on my watch list and hope to slowly scale into the stock on pullbacks, particularly if I can get in when shares are sub-$100. For current investors, I believe this is still a good point to keep holding this name in your portfolio but be mindful of the valuation risk. Although it is still worthy of being in your portfolio, I would not have extensive exposure here, as I do believe it will pullback at some point.

data.title: Medtronic: Several Reasons For Optimism Before The Upcoming Earnings Release
data.description:
Investment thesis

My previous bullish thesis about Medtronic (NYSE:MDT) aged well as the share price gained around 8% since mid-August 2024. The company reports its next earnings soon, on February 18. I am quite optimistic about the upcoming earnings release because the company has a flawless earnings surprise record over the last eight quarters. In addition, there were several positive fundamental developments that reinforce my optimism. The valuation looks attractive, and MTD certainly remains a "Buy".

Earnings preview and recent developments

Medtronic has a flawless earnings surprise record with only dual beats over the last eight quarters, which is a very impressive streak. Past success does not guarantee new success, but there are also several other positive fundamental factors.

The numbers don't lie, and Medtronic's quarterly YoY revenue growth has been accelerating over the last few quarters. And it is not a coincidence, but a result of the management's hard work which is evident from the raised guidance during the FQ2 2025 earnings call. For a company with a market cap north of $100 billion raising guidance is not just optimism - it is confidence based on reliable internal data.

Consistent revenue growth and optimistic guidance revisions had quite a positive effect on MDT's cash generating capacity. The TTM free cash flow chart demonstrates a solid upward trend for the last two years, which is another positive trend that helps to improve the company's financial flexibility.

Keeping the company's financial flexibility strong is crucial because MDT balances between aggressively reinvesting in growth while maintaining robust dividend growth for shareholders. Innovation is the core of MDT's growth strategy, and the R&D spending has also increased over the last couple of years. Therefore, we are highly likely to hear promising forward-looking comments from the management regarding the product pipeline during the upcoming earnings call.

I am optimistic about MDT's potential new product roll-outs because there are several indications that the company's innovation spending is quite efficient. The company's FY2024 "vitality index" (the percentage of revenue generated by products released within the past 36 months) reached 20.7%, which was higher than the FY2025 target of 20%. This is a powerful indication of Medtronic's innovation engine in action.

The company also pursues disruptive innovation like robotics and AI. The surgical robotics space might be a bit under-the-radar secular trend, but the niche is rapidly expanding and is expected to demonstrate a 16.6% CAGR between 2024 and 2030. Medtronic is making major moves with its Hugo surgical robot platform to submit for the FDA approval in 2025. MDT currently trails Intuitive Surgical in this niche, but the roll-out of the Hugo robot might help to narrow the gap in market share.

There is more, as I mentioned, the expansion into AI. Medtronic is not just dipping its toes in the AI waters, but they are applying AI capabilities across their entire business. The AI innovation spans from enhancing MDT's medical devices themselves to improving internal manufacturing processes. I think that by integrating AI into their business, the management is future-proofing the company's long-term prospects. The above pie chart suggests that there are loads of competitors and it is crucial to remain at the cutting edge of medical technology.

Medtronic's balance sheet positions the company well for future growth and innovation. The latest reportable quarter closed with almost $8 billion in total cash and quite low leverage levels. Liquidity ratios are also quite strong, meaning that the company is also well-prepared to weather an unexpected storm.

Lastly, seasonality patterns look promising. According to the last fifteen years' averages, the stock historically performs quite well during Q1. Moreover, success rates are sequentially increasing between January and March.

Valuation update

Wall Street analysts believe that MDT is undervalued by around 4% with the average target price of almost $95. I want to check whether MDT is undervalued or not by looking at its valuation ratios and exercising the DDM approach.

The non-GAAP P/E ratio (both TTM and forward) look quite attractive compared to both historical averages and the sector median. The last five years' average forward non-GAAP P/E ratio is 19.14, which is very close to the sector median. Consensus estimates expect the FY2026 P/E ratio to be $5.82. If I multiply this projected EPS by the multiple of 19.14, I get a fair share price of around $111. From this angle, MDT looks 22% undervalued.

Let me proceed with the DDM approach. I need to calculate the discount rate, which is cost of equity. The CAPM approach makes calculating the cost of equity quite straightforward because I need only three variables which are easily available on the Internet.

MDT's cost of equity is 8.34%. Wall Street analysts expect the FY2026 dividend to be $2.99, which I incorporate in my DDM as I am calculating the target price for the next 12 months. The trickiest assumption is the expected dividend growth rate. This is controversial because some analysts say that it is too risky to apply rates substantially higher than inflation averages. I disagree because MDT delivered an impressive 8.85% dividend growth rate over the last 10 years. Therefore, I think that using the last five years' CAGR of 5.65% as the assumption is fair enough.

According to the DDM exercise, the fair value per share is $111. This is the same as the one that was figured out via the multiplication of the forward P/E ratio by the projected FY2026 EPS.

Risks update

A warning sign is that despite MDT's robust fundamentals and notable undervaluation, insiders are not in a hurry to buy the stock. According to the diagram below, insiders were only selling the stock over the last twelve months. Insider selloffs were not very frequent, though. Insider trading trends are not guarantee, but the absence of insider purchases might indicate that they believe that all the positives are already priced in.

As we saw in recent developments, Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) is by far leading in the surgical robots domain. MDT works hard to grapple against ISRG's leadership in this niche, and there is a possibility that the new Hugo robot might even help MDT to capture some portion of ISRG's market share. However, at the long distance ISRG is positioned stronger to compete and drive innovation. Medtronic's balance sheet is strong and healthy, but ISRG's financial position is even better with a $4 billion cash pile and a 1% leverage ratio.

Bottom line

To conclude, I believe that MDT is still a "Buy". My optimism towards the upcoming earnings release is backed by the company's strong fundamentals and a flawless earnings surprise history over the last several quarters. I like the management's firm commitment to drive innovation, which positions MDT well to remain among the industry's technological leaders. The stock is substantially undervalued as two valuation approaches suggest that the fair share price for MDT is $111.

data.title: SKYX Platforms: All Set To Accelerate Growth
data.description:
We wrote about SKYX Platforms (NASDAQ:SKYX) in October and the shares are up 65% since then, so time for a revisit. First, a quick description of what they're doing as a refresher.

SKYX has developed a patented (97 global patents and trademarks) plug-and-play solution for lighting, centered around its ceiling outlet receptacle, a weight-bearing power plug enabling a host of stuff (light fixtures, ceiling fans, etc.) to be installed safely in seconds without touching hazardous wires.

It has important advantages. It's much easier, safer, and quicker to install. It's also patented and has a chance to become a mandatory standard as it has applied to the NEC (National Electrical Code). See our previous article.

The company works on a razor blade model with the receptacle being the razor and the different products as the blades. There are quite a lot of these:

The company can generate revenues not just from product sales but also from royalties, licensing, subscriptions, monitoring services, and the sale of global country rights.

The proposition is to create a critical mass of installed receptacles, which would generate a stream of recurring revenue through product sales.

Given the inherent advantages of the technology, there's a pretty good chance that they will gain traction. Apart from its application to the NEC, there are several other ways the company is forging market acceptance:

  • New products
  • Licensing
  • Partnering
  • Insurance companies
  • Builders

New products

The most notable one is the Smart Universal Power-Plug & Receptacle which adds smart features like app and voice control, scheduling, and energy-saving modes to the same plug-and-play installation.

A third generation of this product, which is about to be introduced takes this significantly further. It can work with existing smart home solutions and even generate recurring revenue streams from services like communications, fire alarms, home intrusion alerts, emergency response services, and monitoring services:

They also introduced a new patented plug-and-play recessed light solution, which happens to be a multibillion-unit market and the installation ease and safety advantages are considerable here.

Partnerships

The company has licensed to GE Licensing and has strategic partnerships with the likes of Ruee Appliances, EGLO, Golden Lightning, and most recently with JIT Electrical Supply. Retailers like Home Depot (HD), Wayfair (W), Kichler, and Quoizel are also onboard.

Sectors

We think their products are highly relevant for a number of segments, take for instance hotels, here is Lance Shaner, Chairman and CEO of Shaner Hotel Group:

I clearly recognize SKYX’s extreme value proposition for hotels, buildings, and homes, and its significant global growth opportunity. I am now aligned to participate as a significant long-term minded SKYX investor. I strongly believe that SKYX’s game-changing advanced and smart platform technologies will make hotels, buildings, and homes, advanced, smart, and safe instantly, while saving cost, time, and lives.

If you check that link you'll notice that he puts his money where his mouth is, being the lead investor in the company's convertible notes, which have a conversion price of $2 (and he was joined by company directors).

But the building sector also looks to be a promising segment, again the advantages (speed, ease, and safety of installation) are key. The company struck a first deal in this segment with developer Jeremiah Baron Companies and it's also targeting professional installers and electricians.

Management even thinks the advantages of its solutions could encourage insurance companies to recommend them and offer discounts on premiums since they could reduce the incidence of fires, ladder falls, and electrocutions.

Financials

Growth was disappointing in Q3 at just 2.5% as the company is gearing up to be able to deliver products to retail partners like Home Depot and Wayfair and its 60-plus websites. Operating costs are already stable for three quarters:

But the company is still bleeding quite a bit of cash although that too is declining with operational cash outflow declining 39% to $2.6M in Q3.

That graph looks worse than it is as:

  • Ruee is helping SKYX to leverage its trade payables to finance operations, enhance its cash position, and lower its cost of capital. Management refers to this arrangement as the Dell Working Capital Model.
  • At the end of Q3, the company had $13M in cash left and added $11M from the convertible notes sale led by Lance Shaner, CEO of Shaner Hotel Group.

Valuation

The shares are quite a bit more expensive than the last time we looked with 156.9M shares fully diluted at $2 per share producing a market cap of $313.8M and an EV of $289.8M, which is 2.48x FY25 EV/S (FY25 expected sales are $116.8M). That's not cheap with analysts still expecting a $0.17 EPS loss for FY25 (although down from $0.33 expected for FY24).

Conclusion

We're inclined to say that the company has everything in place to be able to ramp up sales, like:

  • A patented product concept that brings real benefits to a wide constituency.
  • A potentially enormous reach with 60-plus websites, huge partners, retail chains, and an important licensee in the form of GE licensing.
  • Financing from a hospitality expert, and some insiders should be enough cash to last until they become cash flow positive.

We do think that the recent rally is already reflecting a considerable degree of the expected revenue ramp so we move our rating to Hold. We could change our rating again when we get a feel for the speed of the revenue ramp and implied operating leverage.

This is by no means a disqualification. We love the company's concept and the resources and partnerships it has marshaled to make it a success, which we think is highly likely, and given the recent run-up in the shares, we're not alone in that assessment.

data.title: Thank You, Airbnb, And Farewell (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

I love Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) and I'm a fan of its app. I like traveling and staying in homes and apartments that make me feel more comfortable than hotels, where, of course, one may enjoy many more services but where the atmosphere tells me that I'm a guest who's there to eventually leave. In Airbnbs, I often feel as if I were in a place similar to my home. To say it all, I'm also planning to start with my wife an Airbnb (we live by the seaside and tourist demand here is high). The ancient Romans used to say, "There is no disputing taste," and so I'm aware that many don't share the same view as mine. However, it seems many people worldwide are using and getting increasingly engaged with Airbnb.

Because of this, I have been a bull of Airbnb since its late 2020 IPO. I liked its business model where strong network effects (guests often become hosts) go along with a lean capital structure and a healthy growth pace. Moreover, the company is truly capital light, which enables it to turn into a free cash flow generative machine. Thirdly, I also think the company can scale and leverage its community to enter into new businesses such as selling experiences and similar offerings. But also, its core business has a lot of room for expansion with large and popular travel destinations such as Germany, Brazil, South Korea, and Japan, which are still showing low signs of penetration. In fact, while Airbnb works in more than 220 countries, it's mainly concentrated in only five: The U.S., France, Spain, Italy, and the U.K.

Airbnb had also shown to be managed by good leadership, especially when, during the COVID crisis, the company pivoted to a cost-efficient structure that made it a profit-making machine as soon as the travel market rebounded. Speaking of this market, I also believe that travel is indeed discretionary spending, but among discretionary expenses, it's one of the most stable ones. As soon as people can afford a home and a car, excess money is spent on experiences, and travel is one of their favorite ones. So, I believe Airbnb is positioned to play a more and more dominant role in the industry.

Nonetheless, today, as the stock jumped 16% on better-than-expected earnings, I sold and locked in my gains. I will explain why I did so.

Airbnb's Q4 Earnings Review

Airbnb's Q4 shareholder letter gives us a snapshot of the company's results that rightly excited investors:

  • Quarterly revenues of $2.5B were up 12% YoY, mainly because of growth in nights stayed than an increase in daily rates
  • Quarterly nights and experiences booked were up 12% YoY to 111M
  • Gross booking value increased by 13% to $17.6B
  • Quarterly EBITDA of $765M was up 4% YoY, with a 31% margin
  • Quarterly FCF was $458M, an 18% FCF margin
  • Quarterly net income was in the positive by $461M, with a 19% margin. A year ago, Airbnb reported net income of -$349M, mainly because of a net loss, including non-recurring tax withholding expenses of around $1B.
  • Quarterly share repurchases of $838M brought the full-year total to $3.4B and helped reduce the share count by 2.6%.

Let's think about two points. First of all, revenues grew more than EBITDA. Secondly, if we don't consider the tax withholding expenses paid last year, the company's net income actually decreased YoY, as we can see from the bar graph below.

Free cash flow is also somewhat stable on 2022 levels when it was $455M. At the same time, the TTM FCF - which offers a better view of its development - is increasing, although the margins are not expanding. It may be that Airbnb has somewhat plateaued its FCF margin at around 40%. It is a good margin, don't get me wrong. But it poses some problems when we need to evaluate the stock.

We could then also add that Airbnb carried out a buyback program that yielded around 3.8%, but the share count only decreased by 2.6% because its SBC program is still quite large (almost $1.5B).

Moving on to the balance sheet, we find one of my favorite aspects of the company. First of all, it holds $10.6B in cash and reports only $2B in long-term debt. So, the company needs no financing. Moreover, it's financed at no interest rate. In fact, it holds more than $5.9B on behalf of its customers and has to report another $1.6B as unearned fees. This is because when guests book a stay on Airbnb, they immediately pay. But Airbnb doesn't pay the host until the first night of the stay. In the time between the two moments, Airbnb holds that cash on behalf of its customers. What does Airbnb do with it? It deploys it in short-term investments, earning interest on "free money." Just to make you understand how big of a deal this is, in Q4, it earned $183M in interest income. In FY2024, the total was $818M. Considering an effective tax rate of 20.5%, we have $650M that fatten the bottom line. Since Airbnb reported $2.65B in yearly net income, we have almost 25% of this coming simply from holding money on behalf of customers. Unearned fees work in a similar way. When a guest books, it pays a fee to Airbnb that will be recognized only once the stay begins. This is money Airbnb already holds, and that is reported as a liability because Airbnb still owes the customer the service it booked. But Airbnb also holds that money and employs it similarly.

So, overall, we have a company with $10.6B in cash, with another $7.5B that generates interest income at almost no cost for the company.

What I think drove the stock up, however, was neither the good results nor the future outlook. Investors expect a lot from Airbnb's announcement that new offerings are coming with the May 2025 Summer Release. Some extra support might have come from the news that Joe Gebbia, the co-founder of Airbnb, is joining Elon Musk's DOGE team. But the real thing many are waiting for: Sponsored listings, which would immediately turn Airbnb into a niche-ad business. So far, Airbnb has said it doesn't want to do that. But sometimes I think about Netflix saying it would never release an ad tier, but it eventually did. I think sponsored listings won't be a new feature this year, but it's only a matter of time (perhaps five years or so) before we will see Airbnb implementing it. For sure, in the last earnings call, Brian Chesky talked about creating an Airbnb ecosystem for cleaning and grocery companies where Airbnb could partner with local businesses. Perhaps it will allow them to offer ads visible to guests.

However, while Wall Street may celebrate today, it probably won't be as pleased when it realizes what Airbnb has already announced: Heavy investments are coming. By heavy, I don't mean that Airbnb will splurge billions. But we saw that already in Q4, its product development costs increased from $432M a year ago to $538M. Sales and marketing also saw a similar increase from $424M a year ago to $547M now. This year, Airbnb said it plans on investing $200M to $250M in its new businesses. So, we're not talking about a huge amount of money, but surely, we will see the company's FCF a bit lower than usual. Part of the negative impact, however, might be offset by applying AI to customer service, which will improve the overall efficiency and cost structure of this branch.

Valuation

While I greatly appreciate the company, my issue now lies with Airbnb's valuation. It's trading at 34x its 2025 earnings. Its fwd EV/EBITDA is close to 19x, and the fwd P/FCF is above 21x. If Airbnb were a rapidly expanding business, these multiples could be somewhat justified. But Airbnb should grow at a slower pace (mid-to-high-single digits). Analysts expect Airbnb to grow its 2026 earnings by 15%, which gives it a 2026 fwd PE of 28. But, we saw that Airbnb doesn't grow its bottom line at the fast pace of its revenues. As a result, Seeking Alpha grades Airbnb's growth with a D-. Right now, Airbnb is priced for perfection, and this gives a little margin of error to its management and its investors. With the competitor Booking Holdings trading at lower multiples, Airbnb right now doesn't seem a very compelling opportunity, but rather a stock that may retrace sharply at the first report that doesn't look exactly as investors expect. Right now, Airbnb bolstered investors' enthusiasm by reporting a strong quarter, with higher-than-expected stays and GBV. But what will happen if Airbnb doesn't report a 10%-plus quarter? A positive catalyst may come if Airbnb is able to grow its FCF at 4% to 5% per year. In fact, right now, Airbnb's EV is $79.12B. If we plug this in a DCF model where Airbnb's 2025 FCF is projected to be $5B and assume a discount rate of 8%, we see that the implied FCF growth rate is around 1.58%. This means that Airbnb needs to prove to Mister Market that it can generate profits at a faster-than-anticipated pace.

So, after I reviewed the report, I saw the stock moving up and up. But it seems to me that the valuation has become too steep. As a result, I took my gains, thanking Airbnb for the value it created for me. Right now, I'm willing to wait on the sidelines for a drop to take place and present me with a new opportunity to seize.

data.title: S&P 500 Earnings: 2025 S&P 500 EPS Estimates Continue To Be Revised Lower
data.description:
There is a lot of focus on the 10-year Treasury yield thanks to comments from President Trump and Treasury Secretary Bessent.

This week the hotter-than-expected core CPI (+0.4% actual vs. +0.3% expected) sent Treasury prices lower, and then they rallied back, only to end the week unchanged. The 10-year Treasury yield closed this Friday, February 14th, 2025 at 4.48%, and closed last Friday, February 7th at 4.48%.

Begin to get anxious about inflation and the Treasury market if the 10-year Treasury closes above the October ’23 high of 4.99-5% on volume.

You’d never guess this, listening to the mainstream financial media.

S&P 500 earnings

  • The forward 4-quarter estimate ended this week at $270.46, versus last week’s $271.24 and January 3rd’s $272.67.
  • The P/E on the forward P/E ratio is now 22.6x vs. 22.2x and January 3rd’s 21.8x
  • The S&P 500 earnings yield is now 4.42%, versus 4.5% last week, and 4.6% as of January 3rd ’25.

Here’s what’s worth watching:

Click on the above spreadsheet and note the rates of change for the expected 2025 S&P 500 EPS estimate.

Part of this weakness is a function of how strong actual Q4 ’24 S&P 500 earnings turned out:

This spreadsheet cut shows the weekly progression in expected Q4 ’24 S&P 500 EPS and revenue growth, starting with the beginning of the quarter. Q4 ’24 earnings unofficially ends this week with Walmart’s (WMT) earnings, but readers can see how strong the “upside surprise” was for both Q4 S&P 500 EPS and revenue.

The point being that 2025 estimates will naturally be cut a little more based on the strong Q4 ’24.

As it stands today, 2024 S&P 500 EPS and expected 2025 EPS are both expected to grow 11% each year. 2024 EPS has been revised higher in the second half of calendar 2024 from 9%, while expected 2025 S&P 500 EPS growth has been revised lower from 14-15% last October ’24 to 10% as of this weekend. (Click on the spreadsheet below to see the expected growth for the S&P 500 EPS from 2024-2026.)

Summary/conclusion

While this might cause some readers some anxiety or alarm, the fact that we have a new administration, with a whole new set of tariff initiatives not to mention other initiatives, is likely causing some angst on the part of analysts that might be unsure how to factor in healthcare policy commentary, defense industry commentary, and many other aspects of the new administration.

While the megacap 7 or megacap 10 have seen some pressure on 2025 revenue estimates, the negative revisions are not yet too concerning.

A problem would arise if we started seeing negative pre-announcements, from which we could infer that companies are seeing economic conditions deteriorate faster than their previous guidance expected.

Corporate credit spreads continue to remain well bid. Even with inflation uncertainty and a little Treasury volatility, credit spreads continue to grind lower. That’s a plus for the economy.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Investing can and does involve the loss of principal, even for short periods of time. None of the above information may be updated, and if updated, may not be done in a timely fashion.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Vistagen: Pherine Candidate Proof Of Concept Continues With Fifth Indication
data.description:
Vistagen Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:VTGN) has been doing well to progress the use of its lead candidate fasedienol for the treatment of patients with Social Anxiety Disorder [SAD]. This would be based on the facts that I laid out in a previous Seeking Alpha article entitled “VistaGen: Positive Data Leads To Catalyst In 2024 And Beyond.” In this particular article, I noted about the positive results it had achieved from its ongoing phase 2 PALISADE-2 study using this drug to treat these patients with SAD. Well, since the release of positive data from this mid-stage study, I'm pleased to report that it has been able to initiate two phase 3 studies under the PALISADE program. In particular, it has already been able to begin two late-stage studies using fasedienol to treat these patients.

Both of these phase 3 studies are known as PALISADE-3 and PALISADE-4 respectively. Not only that, but another item to note is that it has also been able to start dosing patients in a repeat dosing study. This is going to help inform the ability to decide upon future expanded label use. Why do I bring up this program now, though? It is because there actually is a catalyst opportunity for investors to focus on. That is, it is expected to release data from both of these phase 3 PALISADE studies during 2025. However, for the intent of this article, I believe that it is important to go over another product candidate from its pipeline, this would be the ongoing development and proof-of-concept from its fifth pherine drug known as PH284.

This particular candidate was tested in a phase 2 study targeting patients with Wasting Syndrome or Cachexia. Vistagen was able to release positive data from this study showing that this drug outshined placebo when it came to the Subjective Feeling Of Hunger [SFH] endpoint by a wide margin. The main premise of this biotech here is that it has massive potential because this is the 5th pherine product candidate from its pipeline, which has been able to help treat patients with neuropsychiatric disorders.

PH284 For The Treatment Of Patients With Cachexia

As I stated above at the beginning of this article, Vistagen has not only been able to report positive results from its phase 2 PALISADE-2 study using its drug fasedienol for the treatment of patients with Social Anxiety Disorder [SAD]. It is already in the process of running two phase 3 studies as part of the PALISADE program. Investors can expect data from both of these late-stage trials in 2025, which, if positive, are likely to cause the stock price to trade higher. However, the promise of this company lies in the fact that it can use its scientific research to develop nasal sprays that are to be used for the treatment of a wide variety of neuropsychiatric disorders.

With that said, this is where a 5th candidate has been able to establish proof-of-concept from the company's pipeline. This is the development of nasal spray PH284 comes into play because this was just evaluated in an exploratory phase 2a study targeting patients with Cachexia. Before going over the positive data released from this particular mid-stage study, plus any catalysts to be released from it, it is first important to note what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for it could be.

Cachexia [Also known as Wasting Syndrome] is a type of disorder characterized as the loss of fat or muscle. Why does this occur? It occurs because of patients being diagnosed with devastating disorders like cancer, AIDS, HIV, and advance heart disease as well. One of the hallmarks of this disease is that patients lose fat and muscle because of lack of hunger. Thus, a drug like PH284 is being designed to be given at least 10 minutes before a meal to increase hunger for these patients. What I'm going to show below is the positive phase 2a proof-of-concept data that was released, which indicated that there was a massive improvement in margin in terms of Subjective Feeling Of Hunger [SFH].

The global cancer cachexia market size is projected to reach $3.76 billion by 2032. This will be a huge market opportunity, especially since this is a huge unmet medical need. How so? There are no FDA approved treatments or drugs to help these patients with cachexia. Not only that, but approximately 80% of advanced cancer patients eventually have cachexia. The last item to note is that this disorder contributes to 30% of cancer deaths. As you can see, not only is this a crucial study for the company to generate shareholder value, but one in which that could change the scope of treating these patients with cachexia.

To see if PH284 could be used effectively as a pherine nasal spray to treat patients with Cancer Cachexia, the company was able to evaluate its use in a phase 2a proof-of-concept study. Before going over this particular study, one key item to note is that this drug was obtained with the acquisition of Pherin in February 2023. With that said, this mid-stage trial was a randomized, double-blind placebo-controlled one, whereby a total of 40 women with terminal cancer and cachexia were recruited into it. To truly see if this intranasal drug would be able to help treat these patients, this phase 2a trial was designed in such a way to establish a baseline in terms of placebo right off the bat. That is, it was noted that patients from Day 1 through Day 4 would first receive placebo 30 minutes before each meal. However, once this evaluation period was completed, patients were then randomized 1:1 to receive either of the following doses:

  • PH284
  • Placebo.

The dosing evaluation of this study was set up in a strong way because patients were administered 0.4 µg/50 µL in each nostril for a total dosing of 3.2 µg 4 times daily. That is, this level of dosing was given before each of the 4 meals throughout the day [breakfast, mid-morning snack, lunch, and dinner]. The main efficacy deployed for this study was that of “Subjective Feeling of Hunger” 10 minutes before each meal.

This was an exploratory phase 2a proof-of-concept study, but the drug itself definitely should be investigated further. That's because the outcome was that the patients given intra-nasal PH284 achieved SFH improvement of 71% from baseline prior to Dinner on Day 7. This number in itself is quite impressive, but what's even more substantial based on this efficacy data, is that the patients who were given placebo instead only saw a 1% improvement of SFH from baseline on this measurement. With no FDA approved therapies for treating these patients with Cachexia, this is another candidate in the pipeline that could be achieved a “first” for patients. While fasedienol for the treatment of patients with SAD won't establish a “first” per se, what it can do is target a huge underserved patient population. This is because many people with SAD either avoid current therapies or stop taking them altogether. This is due to lack of efficacy for starters, but other contributing factors would be that they have heavy side effects and also have the potential to become addictive as well.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Vistagen had cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $88.6 million as of December 31st of 2024. The reason for the cash on hand is because of an underwritten public offering of $100 million it was able to complete back in October 2023. With this newly received cash, plus what it had on hand, it was able to not only start the phase 3 PALISADE program, but continue to run several other studies from its pipeline.

With that said, it is my opinion that it might need to raise funds at some point in the 2nd half of 2025. This is based on the notion of what the company notes in its 10-Q SEC Filing, which is that it believes that it has enough cash on hand for a cash runway to fund its operations for more than 12 months. Just how much over is not clear, but it is likely going to need to raise additional cash nonetheless.

The company burns roughly $15.3 million in cash per quarter. This is broken down into $11.3 million in R&D expenses and then $4 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Vistagen. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing PALISADE program, which has deployed the two late-stage studies using fasedienol for the treatment of patients with Social Anxiety Disorder. Again, these two studies are PALISADE-3 and PALISADE-4. The risk here is that there is no assurance that upon the release of data from one or both of these clinical studies, that they are going to meet the primary endpoint with statistical significance for starters. Nor, that even if the primary endpoint of one or both studies is met, that the company will be allowed to file a regulatory application to seek marketing approval of fasedienol for these patients.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the phase 2a proof-of-concept study using PH284 for the treatment of patients with cancer cachexia. That's because this was an exploratory study. It is highly likely that this biotech will need to be able to run another late-stage study to eventually seek U.S. marketing approval of this drug candidate for the treatment of these patients. There is no assurance that data to eventually be released from such a phase 3 pivotal study will turn out to be positive. Not only that, but the study that reported positive data was conducted in Mexico. The company will likely have to be able to do some clinical work to obtain IND clearance to run a phase 2 study for this program in the United States. Thus, another risk here is that there is no assurance that it will receive such clearance or Authorization from the FDA to do so.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the financial situation that this biotech is in. Even though it states that it has over 12 months of cash to fund itself, it is likely going to need to start investigating how to raise funds. Thus, the risk here is that it may decide to dilute shareholders, either through further selling of its common stock or by some other financial instrument. There is no guarantee that it will be able to obtain financing on good terms, nor that it will be able to have enough cash on hand to continue to develop all drugs in its pipeline.

Conclusion

Vistagen has done well to develop the use of its lead pherine nasal spray fasedienol for the treatment of patients with Social Anxiety Disorder. It was able to achieve the primary endpoint of the phase 2 PALISADE-2 study with statistical significance regarding the SUDS score. This is a scoring system used to measure disease severity in patients with this disorder. Not only was the primary endpoint met, but the secondary endpoint of CGI-I scale score was also met. The company is already being proactive in that it had also initiated a phase 2 repeat dose study as well. Again, the purpose of this is to eventually expand the label to include further dosing for these patients. This is a good long-term company to own, and the reason I state that is because of what it has been able to do. It has shown that its intra-nasal spray pherine drugs can help treat patients with various neuropsychiatric disorders.

Matter of fact, this goes back to the notion about what I stated above, in that the targeting of the cancer cachexia patients was the 5th proof-of-concept indication established for it. I haven't even gone over other promising programs from its pipeline that could also be used to generate shareholder value. One such program would be the development of Itruvone for the treatment of patients with Major Depressive Disorder [MDD] and PH80 for vasomotor symptoms [hot flashes] due to menopause. It established positive phase 2a studies for both of these indications using pherines.

The promising differentiation of the Itruvone pherine drug development program would be the ability to give it to MDD patients as a non-systemic treatment option without all the unwanted side effects, weight gain and safety issues with current depression therapies. With the expected data readouts of two of the phase 3 PALISADE studies using fasedienol for the treatment of patients with SAD in 2025, plus the promising phase 2a proof-of-concept study data achieved in targeting patients with cancer cachexia using PH284, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.
